Open Source AI.titleZh: OpenAI's GPT-5.5 is here, and it's no potato: narrowly beats Anthropic's Claude Mythos Preview on Terminal-Bench 2.0 - VentureBeat
Open Source AI.content: # OpenAI's GPT-5.5 is here, and it's no potato: narrowly beats Anthropic's Claude Mythos Preview on Terminal-Bench 2.0 [Carl Franzen](/author/carlfranzen) After months of rumors and [reports](https://apnews.com/article/openai-chatgpt-spud-sam-altman-anthropic-mythos-3c2674f5cdf67ac6d88eedb207de117c) that OpenAI was developing a new, more powerful AI large language model for use in ChatGPT and through its application programming interface (API), allegedly codenamed "Spud" internally, the company has today unveiled its latest offering under the more formal name [GPT-5.5](https://openai.com/index/introducing-gpt-5-5/). And to likely no one's surprise, it's hardly a "potato" in the disparaging sense of the word: GPT-5.5 retakes the lead for OpenAI in generally available LLMs, coming ahead of rivals Anthropic's and Google's latest public offerings, and even beating the private Anthropic Claude Mythos Preview model narrowly on one benchmark (essentially a statistical tie). "It’s definitely our strongest model yet on coding, both measured by benchmarks and based on the feedback that we’ve gotten from trusted partners, as well as our own experience," explained Amelia "Mia" Glaese, VP of Research at OpenAI, in a video call with journalists ahead of the launch earlier today. OpenAI positions GPT-5.5 as a fundamental redesign of how intelligence interacts with a computer's operating system and professional software stacks. "What is really special about this model is how much more it can do with less guidance," said OpenAI co-founder and president Greg Brockman on the same call. "It’s way more intuitive to use. It can look at an unclear problem and figure out what needs to happen next." Brockman proceeded to emphasize the areas in which users can expect to see gains from using GPT-5.5 compared to OpenAI's prior state-of-the-art model, GPT-5.4, which remains available (for now) to users and enterprises at half the API cost of its new successor. "It’s extremely good at coding," Brockman said of GPT-5.5. "It’s also great at broader computer work, computer use, scientific research—these kinds of applications that are very intelligent bottlenecks." [OpenAI CEO and-cofounder Sam Altman also weighed in](https://x.com/sama/status/2047379615589777666?s=20) on the launch and the company's philosophy in a post on X, writing, in part: "We want our users to have access to the best technology and for everyone to have equal opportunity." The model is available in two variants: GPT-5.5 and GPT-5.5 Pro, distinguished by the latter offering enhanced precision and specialized logic for handling the most rigorous cognitive demands. While the standard version serves as the versatile flagship for general intelligence tasks, the Pro model is architected specifically for high-stakes environments such as legal research, data science, and advanced business analytics where accuracy is paramount. This premium tier provides noticeably more comprehensive and better-structured responses, supported by specialized latency optimizations that ensure high-quality performance during complex, multi-step workflows. Unfortunately for third-party software developers, API access is not yet available for either GPT-5.5 nor GPT-5.5 Pro and will be coming "very soon," according to the company's announcement blog post. "API deployments require different safeguards and we are working closely with partners and customers on the safety and security requirements for serving it at scale," OpenAI writes. For the time being, GPT-5.5 is available only to paying subscribers of the ChatGPT Plus ($20 monthly), Pro ($100-$200 monthly), Business, and Enterprise users, with GPT-5.5 Pro access starting at the Pro tier and upwards. ## **A focus on agency** At the core of GPT-5.5 is a focus on "agentic" performance—specifically in coding, computer use, and scientific research. Unlike its predecessors, which often required granular, step-by-step prompting to avoid "hallucinating" a path forward, GPT-5....

"We get used to these incredible tools only to have them taken away, replaced by a worse or stranger model, and then eventually become outpriced," a user wrote on the Anthropic subreddit. Increasingly, company representatives are stating that agentic AI has outstripped their initial plans. Thanks to [OpenClaw and similar tools](https://www.businessinsider.com/openclaw-creator-moment-ai-changed-everything-2026-4), power AI users have found ways to run AI models around the clock. As a result, companies' already limited amount of available compute is being taxed in ways it wasn't six months ago. "Long-running, parallelized sessions now regularly consume far more resources than the original plan structure was built to support," Joe Binder, VP of Product, wrote in a blog post for GitHub announcing its new limitations. Binder said that "it is now common" for some AI requests "to incur costs that exceed the plan price." These aren't the economics that AI companies signed up for. Open Source AI.title: White House memo claims mass AI theft by Chinese firms - BBC
Open Source AI.titleZh: White House memo claims mass AI theft by Chinese firms - BBC
Open Source AI.content: [Skip to content](#bbc-main) [Watch Live](/watch-live-news/) [Watch Live](/watch-live-news/) # White House memo claims mass AI theft by Chinese firms Kali HaysTechnology reporter The White House has said it will work more closely with US artificial intelligence (AI) firms to combat "industrial-scale campaigns" by foreign actors to steal advances in the technology. Michael Kratsios, Director of Science and Technology Policy, wrote in an internal memo that the administration had new information indicating "foreign entities, principally based in China" were exploiting American firms. Through a process called "distilling", such firms are essentially copying AI technology developed by US companies, he said. A representative of China's US embassy in Washington DC said its development was "the result of its own dedication and effort as well as international cooperation". In the memo, Kratsios said the aim was to "systematically undermine American research and development and access proprietary information". In an attempt avoid and halt "malicious exploitation," he said the White House will be doing four things: * sharing more information with US AI companies about "tactics employed and actors involved" in distillation campaigns * working to "better coordinate" with companies to fight the attacks * develop a set of "best practices to identify, mitigate, and remediate" them * "explore" how the White House can hold foreign actors accountable for such distillation The memo did not detail any specific plans for action against foreign entities found to be undertaking distillation of US AI technology. A White House spokesperson declined to comment beyond the memo. A representative of China's US embassy in Washington DC took issue with "the unjustified suppression of Chinese companies by the US" in response to the memo "China is not only the world's factory but is also becoming the world's innovation lab," the representative added. "China's development is the result of its own dedication and effort as well as international cooperation that delivers mutual benefits." Distillation campaigns are carried out by firms that usually operate many thousands of individual accounts for a given AI chatbot or tool, allowing them to appear as normal users. Those accounts then undertake more coordinated attempts to "jailbreak" or otherwise expose information about AI models that is not supposed to be made public, which is saved and applied to their own AI model building and training. "As methods to detect and mitigate industrial-scale distillation grow more sophisticated, foreign entities who build their AI capabilities on such fragile foundations should have little confidence in the integrity and reliability of the models they produce," Kratsios said. While Kratsios did not name any foreign entities, leading AI companies like OpenAI and Anthropic have said they are dealing with such distillation activity. Earlier this year, Anthropic described distillation "attacks" by three AI laboratories, DeepSeek, Moonshot, and MiniMax, saying it had found them all to be working to copy Anthropic models through distillation campaigns. All three of the labs are based in China. OpenAI has also accused DeepSeek of copying its technology. DeepSeek, Moonshot, and MiniMax did not immediately respond to BBC requests for comment. DeepSeek was initially released last year and quickly became one of the most popular AI models and chatbots among users. The firm said at the time that the model cost only a few million dollars to create, a fraction of the hundreds of billions of dollars being spent by other AI firms to build their models and tools. Last month, the DeepSeek chatbot suffered a major outage. It is expected to soon release a new version of its AI model. US President Donald Trump is expected to visit China in May. Open Source AI.content: Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles and JavaScript. * [View all journals](https://www.nature.com/siteindex) * [Search](#search-menu) * [Log in](https://idp.nature.com/auth/personal/springernature?redirect_uri=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41596-026-01364-8?error=cookies_not_supported&code=a305968c-7b53-4c25-9fd5-c2ff13aef2e3) * [Content Explore content](#explore) * [About the journal](#about-the-journal) * [Publish with us](#publish-with-us) * [Subscribe](/nprot/subscribe) * [Sign up for alerts](https://journal-alerts.springernature.com/subscribe?journal_id=41596) * [RSS feed](https://www.nature.com/nprot.rss) * Protocol * Published: # Discovery of candidate therapeutic targets with Geneformer * [Yujie Zhang  (张毓杰)](#auth-Yujie-Zhang-Aff1-Aff2-Aff3)  [ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-1017-0316](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1017-0316)[1](#Aff1),[2](#Aff2),[3](#Aff3), * [Madhavan S. Venkatesh](#auth-Madhavan_S_-Venkatesh-Aff1-Aff2-Aff4)[1](#Aff1),[2](#Aff2),[4](#Aff4) & * [Christina V. Theodoris](#auth-Christina_V_-Theodoris-Aff1-Aff2-Aff3-Aff5)  [ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0003-1658-1447](https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1658-1447)[1](#Aff1),[2](#Aff2),[3](#Aff3),[5](#Aff5) [*Nature Protocols*](/nprot) (2026)[Cite this article](#citeas) ### Subjects * [Computational models](/subjects/computational-models) * [Machine learning](/subjects/machine-learning) ## Abstract Mapping the connections between genes enables the identification of networks disrupted in disease. The approach, however, requires large amounts of data, making the discovery of therapeutic targets difficult in settings with limited data. We recently developed a foundational artificial intelligence model, Geneformer, pretrained on a large-scale corpus of single-cell transcriptomes (initially ~30 million, now >100 million) to enable context-aware predictions in network biology with limited data. Here, we cover the methodology for using Geneformer through a combination of zero-shot inference, fine-tuning and in silico perturbation. The procedure includes the tokenization of raw gene expression counts into rank value encodings aligned with the model’s pretrained vocabulary. Separability of relevant phenotypes in the pretrained embedding space is first assessed with zero-shot embeddings. Fine-tuning is then performed either with a single task, for example, disease prediction within a specific cell type, or with multiple tasks to jointly learn cross-informative features, such as cell types and disease states. Performance is evaluated with confusion matrices, macro F1 scores and embedding analysis. Subsequently, in silico perturbation simulates gene repression or activation and quantifies the shift in cell state embeddings, prioritizing candidate targets by statistical and biological metrics. The approach also supports perturbation using a quantized model to enhance computational efficiency. Outputs include predictive models fine-tuned for context-specific cell state representations and rank-ordered predictions of perturbations to induce each target state. The full pipeline typically completes in under 2 days on a standard GPU workstation and requires only moderate Python experience. ## Key points * Geneformer uses a bidirectional transformer model to learn patterns of covariation between genes. The protocol includes single-task and multi-task learning, with options for improved scalability, and can be optimized depending on the research question and dataset size. * The protocol provides a framework for fine-tuning and in silico perturbation analysis, serving as an alternative to pretrained foundation models including scGen, scGPT, scFoundation, GeneCompass, UCE, Nicheformer, scSimilarity and TranscriptFormer....

Infra & Chips.title: Nvidia Joins Funding Round for Vast Data at $30 Billion Valuation in AI Storage Push - MLQ.ai
Infra & Chips.titleZh: Nvidia Joins Funding Round for Vast Data at $30 Billion Valuation in AI Storage Push - MLQ.ai
Infra & Chips.content: # Nvidia Joins Funding Round for Vast Data at $30 Billion Valuation in AI Storage Push April 23, 2026 at 9:44 AM • by MLQ Agent #### Key Points * Vast Data secured $1 billion in Series F funding at a $30 billion valuation, up from $9.1 billion in 2023[1][2]. * Drive Capital and Access Industries led the round, with Nvidia, Fidelity, and NEA participating[1][2]. * Company reports $4 billion in cumulative bookings, over $500 million in annual recurring revenue, and positive free cash flow[1]. * Serves AI clients including xAI, CoreWeave, and U.S. Air Force[2]. * Round includes primary and secondary capital, with over $500 million to early investors and employees[1]. Vast Data raised approximately $1 billion in a Series F funding round, achieving a $30 billion valuation more than three times its 2023 value of $9.1 billion. The round, led by Drive Capital and Access Industries, included participation from Nvidia, Fidelity Management & Research Company, and NEA amid rising demand for AI data infrastructure[1][2]. ## Funding Round Details The Series F round combines primary and secondary capital totaling about $1 billion, with more than $500 million directed to early investors and employees selling holdings. This values Vast Data at $30 billion, making it Israel's most valuable private tech company following Wiz's $32 billion sale to Google. Existing investors like Nvidia joined new and continuing backers in the transaction[1][2]. ## Financial Performance Vast Data reported $4 billion in cumulative bookings and over $500 million in committed annual recurring revenue at the end of its latest fiscal year. The company operates with positive margins and free cash flow, reflecting strong growth in AI-optimized data platforms[1]. ## Company Background and Customers Founded in 2016 and based in New York with Israeli roots, Vast Data provides software for storing and processing large data volumes used in AI training and operations. Customers include xAI, CoreWeave, and the U.S. Air Force, highlighting its role in enterprise AI deployments[1][2]. ## Nvidia's AI Stack Expansion Nvidia's investment in Vast Data underscores its broadening strategy beyond GPU hardware into the full AI infrastructure stack, including data storage critical for handling massive datasets in training large language models. By backing a company with proven financial metrics like $500 million in annual recurring revenue and free cash flow positivity, Nvidia positions itself to strengthen ecosystem partnerships that drive demand for its chips. This move aligns with industry trends where chipmakers invest in complementary technologies to sustain AI growth[1][2]. The $30 billion valuation, tripling from 2023, signals investor confidence in data platform providers amid the AI buildout. Vast Data's focus on high-performance storage differentiates it in a market where enterprises require scalable solutions for AI workloads. Participation from diverse investors like Drive Capital, Access Industries, Fidelity, and NEA indicates broad recognition of Vast's execution, particularly its $4 billion in bookings serving high-profile AI firms[1]. ## AI Infrastructure Scaling Vast Data's funding positions it to accelerate product development and expand market reach as AI infrastructure demand intensifies. With established clients like xAI and CoreWeave, the company can leverage its cash flow positivity to capture more enterprise deployments, potentially pushing annual recurring revenue past current levels toward multibillion-dollar scale. Nvidia's involvement may foster integrated solutions combining Vast's storage with Nvidia hardware, enhancing adoption[1][2]. Future growth hinges on sustained AI investment cycles and Vast's ability to maintain margins amid competition. As the most valuable private Israeli tech firm, it faces pressure to deliver an IPO or acquisition, though secondary sales provide liquidity without immediate public market pressures. Infra & Chips.title: Eurobites: BT sets out its 'sovereign' stall - Light Reading
Infra & Chips.titleZh: Eurobites: BT sets out its 'sovereign' stall - Light Reading
Infra & Chips.content: * [AI & Machine Learning](/digital-transformation/ai-machine-learning) * [Business Transformation](/business-management/business-transformation) * [Data Centers](/it-infrastructure/data-centers) * [Satellite](/wireless/satellite) * [News](/latest-news) # Eurobites: BT sets out its 'sovereign' stall Also in today's EMEA regional roundup: how the UK planning system is derailing AI; Middle East, Africa drives Q1 growth for Orange; MTN deploys Huawei's massive MIMO in Nigeria. [Paul Rainford,](/author/paul-rainford)Assistant Editor, Europe,Light Reading April 23, 2026 3 Min Read (Source: BT) UK incumbent operator BT has announced a deal with Nscale to set up data centers as part of a wider push into the "sovereign" AI market. The data centers will be powered by Nvidia's infrastructure and, says BT, will help organizations adopt AI securely, under UK control. The plan is for Nscale to build up to 14 megawatts of AI data center infrastructure across three existing BT sites in the UK. BT will provide connectivity to these new data centers, expanding the UK's AI compute capacity. In a related move, BT Business is announcing what it is trumpeting as the UK's "first full suite of sovereign services" for public and private sector organizations. The company cites a [new report](https://www.bt.com/content/dam/bt-plc/assets/documents/about-bt/policy-and-regulation/download-centre/2026/uks-digital-sovereignty-opportunity.pdf) it commissioned from Assembly Research which claims that concerns over data security are putting the brakes on AI adoption in the UK. The report adds that accelerated investment in UK-based data centers could generate £14.6 billion (US$19.7 billion) by 2030, while the wider availability of sovereign cloud services is estimated to be worth an additional £13.6 billion ($18.3 billion) over the next five years. ## How the UK planning system is derailing AI Another potential obstacle to the rollout of sovereign data center-driven AI in the UK, it seems, is the country's planning system. At least that's what is being suggested in a [new study](https://more.hoarelea.com/societal-insights-powering-places/) from engineering consultancy firm Hoare Lea which found, among other things, that the average time to secure planning consent for a new data center was 490 days, with the longest planning time being just over five years. Many of the planning applications faced objections from the public over such issues as a lack of community engagement and perceived environmental impacts. The study concludes there needs to be a reset in how the data center industry communicates with local communities and stakeholders and it ensures that renewable energy is a key part of planning strategy going forward. ## Middle East, Africa drives Q1 growth for Orange Double-digit growth in Orange's Africa & Middle East segment helped drive revenues up at the French giant by 3.5% year-over-year to €10.09 billion ($11.78 billion) in the first quarter. EBITDAaL (earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortization, after leases) reached €2.6 billion ($3.03 billion), up 6.6%, though this was helped by what Orange calls "a positive wholesale effect in France," without which EBITDAaL growth would have been approximately 3.5%. Orange now lays claim to 9.3 million "converged" customers in Europe, an increase of 1.9% on the previous year. The quarter marked the launch of Orange's "Trust the Future" plan, which CEO Christel Heydemann presented to the world in February. (See [Orange puts trust at heart of new strategic plan](/digital-transformation/orange-puts-trust-at-heart-of-new-strategic-plan).) ## MTN deploys Huawei's massive MIMO in Nigeria MTN Nigeria and Huawei have deployed what they claim is the world's first commercial sub-1GHz massive MIMO site. The setup, says Huawei, increases lowband LTE traffic by 104%, boosts downlink user-perceived rate by 28% and reduces physical resource block usage by 8% compared with previous 4T4R equipment. ## Eutelsat ...

Infra & Chips.title: Equinix's AI Discovery Hub set to hasten AI deployment in HK - Light Reading
Infra & Chips.titleZh: Equinix's AI Discovery Hub set to hasten AI deployment in HK - Light Reading
Infra & Chips.content: * [AI & Machine Learning](/digital-transformation/ai-machine-learning) * [Data Centers](/it-infrastructure/data-centers) * [Digital Transformation](/digital-transformation) * [News](/latest-news) # Equinix's AI Discovery Hub set to accelerate AI deployment in Hong Kong Equinix has partnered with HPE to create an Nvidia-powered AI testing environment that will help companies in Hong Kong speed up their AI deployments. [Gigi Onag,](/author/gigi-onag)Senior Editor, APAC,Light Reading April 23, 2026 3 Min Read (Source: Ruslan Bardash on Unsplash) Equinix has forged a strategic collaboration with HPE to establish an AI Discovery Hub in Hong Kong that is designed to help companies build, deploy and scale AI securely across hybrid multi-cloud environments. According to Equinix, the AI Discovery Hub is designed to help companies overcome the challenges they face when moving from an AI pilot to a production environment. “Hong Kong is at an inflection point where AI is moving from experimentation into day-to-day operations. That shift shows up immediately in how much data enterprises need to move and the speed at which they need to move it," Joanne Hon, Equinix Hong Kong managing director, said in a [statement](https://newsroom.equinix.com/2026-04-23-Equinix-Introduces-AI-Discovery-Hub-to-Accelerate-AI-Innovation-and-Deployment-in-Hong-Kong-with-HPE-and-NVIDIA) on Thursday. She pointed out that AI deployments often stall due to practical constraints. These constraints include siloed data and infrastructure, limited AI-ready power and cooling, latency and throughput bottlenecks when moving large datasets, fragmented access to clouds and partners, and difficult-to-implement governance requirements across distributed environments. Related:[Eurobites: BT sets out its 'sovereign' stall](/ai-machine-learning/eurobites-bt-sets-out-its-sovereign-stall) "This is exactly the gap this collaboration addresses. Combining Equinix’s interconnected, AI-ready infrastructure and ecosystem with an AI factory at-scale from HPE and Nvidia AI infrastructure and software offers customers a clear path to deploy AI closer to their data and users," Hon added. ##### AI testing environment Equinix stated that the AI Discovery Hub is an open AI testing environment that will provide an AI-ready foundation where datasets, models, and ecosystem partners can converge close to clouds and networks. This foundation is supported by automation, which reduces manual effort and enables proactive optimization. Companies can use the environment to test and validate AI applications before deploying them on a large scale. This allows them to evaluate performance, compatibility, and operational workflows under real-world conditions while maintaining low latency. The AI Discovery Hub will be hosted at HK6, Equinix's AI-ready facility in Tsuen Wan. HK6 is equipped with direct-to-chip liquid cooling technology, which supports GPUs that far exceed the thermal limits of traditional air-cooled data centers. The AI Discovery Hub will offer HPE's AI factory at scale solution, providing a complete infrastructure, software and service stack that enables organizations to design, build and operate AI architectures and workflows at any scale to accelerate time to AI value.  HPE's AI factory at scale solution combines Nvidia AI software, accelerated infrastructure, and high-performance, low-latency networking. It also has built-in guardrails and governance modules that support secure, efficient, and traceable AI operations. Related:[Verizon CEO emerges as telecom's AI doomster-in-chief](/ai-machine-learning/verizon-ceo-emerges-as-telecom-s-ai-doomster-in-chief) The AI Discovery Hub will feature enterprise-grade agentic AI guardrails through Nvidia GPUs, Nvidia Nemo Claw, and the Nvidia Agent Toolkit. This open-source reference stack is designed to make OpenClaw autonomous AI agents safer with policy-based controls. It includes Nvidia OpenShell, which enforces policy-based privacy and security, ...

Despite this growth, a large portion of the revenue is captured by global platforms like Google One, Facebook, ChatGPT, and YouTube. Domestic apps like JioHotstar and SonyLIV also perform well in video streaming. The Indian app market, although mature in downloads, is still evolving in monetization, with a revenue per download of $0.03 compared to Southeast Asia’s $0.20. Additionally, India’s AI sector is rapidly expanding, with the country ranked as one of the top AI markets globally. However, usage is concentrated in a few cities, and infrastructure challenges persist, with over $200 billion needed for data storage investments to support further growth. India’s future in AI hinges on strengthening infrastructure alongside application development. #### Tags: [AI growth](https://www.startupecosystem.ca/news/tag/ai-growth/)[Global platforms](https://www.startupecosystem.ca/news/tag/global-platforms/)[Global startup news](https://www.startupecosystem.ca/news/tag/global-startup-news/)[India app market](https://www.startupecosystem.ca/news/tag/india-app-market/)[Infrastructure challenges](https://www.startupecosystem.ca/news/tag/infrastructure-challenges/) ## Infra & Chips.titleZh: Aramark launches platform for AI data center workforce services - Investing.com
Infra & Chips.content: # Aramark launches platform for AI data center workforce services By [Company News](https://www.investing.com/news/company-news) Published 04/23/2026, 07:41 AM Aramark launches platform for AI data center workforce services [View all comments (0)0](#comments-4632119) © Reuters. [ARMK2.81%](https://www.investing.com/equities/aramark-holdings) PHILADELPHIA - Aramark (NYSE:ARMK) announced Wednesday the launch of Aramark Nexus, a platform providing hospitality and workforce support services for hyperscale AI data centers and remote operating environments. The $11.9 billion company is a prominent player in the Hotels, Restaurants & Leisure industry, according to [InvestingPro](https://www.investing.com/pro/ARMK) data. The company disclosed it has secured a multi-year contract with an unnamed global hyperscaler to deliver hospitality and facilities services across multiple AI data center locations. Revenue from the agreement will begin in the current fiscal year, according to a press release statement. Shares have responded positively to growth initiatives, with the stock delivering a 38.9% return over the past year and trading near its 52-week high. The initial engagement involves a workforce campus spanning hundreds of acres supporting thousands of workers. Services include employee housing, dining facilities, transportation to construction sites, and housekeeping operations managed under a unified structure. "We see substantial long-term growth potential in hyperscale digital infrastructure," said John Zillmer, Aramark’s Chief Executive Officer. "Aramark Nexus extends our proven remote operations model into a new, strategically important sector." The company generated $18.8 billion in revenue over the last twelve months with 7% growth, and [InvestingPro](https://www.investing.com/pro/ARMK) analysis indicates the stock is currently undervalued relative to its Fair Value. The platform addresses operational needs created when AI data center development requires skilled workers to live and work in remote locations for extended periods. Services encompass housing, dining, transportation, wellness, security, and daily life support delivered through a single operating model. Aramark Nexus draws on the company’s experience operating in remote environments including offshore energy installations, mining operations, and national parks. The initial engagement is based in the United States, though the platform is designed to scale globally. The company did not disclose financial terms of the contract or identify the hyperscaler client. Aramark operates in 16 countries, providing food and facilities management services to educational institutions, corporations, healthcare providers, and municipalities. In other recent news, Aramark Holdings has been the subject of several analyst updates. UBS has raised its price target for Aramark to $48 from $47, maintaining a Buy rating, citing potential catalysts for the company in the coming months. BofA Securities reinstated coverage with a Buy rating and set a price target of $50, highlighting growth from net new customer wins and high retention rates. Stifel also increased its price target to $47 from $44, following a solid quarter where Aramark showed strong contracting strength and historical highs in retention rates. Similarly, Morgan Stanley adjusted its price target to $45 from $44, noting the company’s organic revenue growth of 5.0%, which exceeded estimates. These developments reflect positive analyst sentiment regarding Aramark’s financial performance and growth prospects. *This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. Please check back later. [Business](https://www.cnn.com/business) 5 min read FOR SUBSCRIBERS # There are fixes for AI’s toll on the power grid. Here’s why they’re not happening By [Ella Nilsen](https://www.cnn.com/profiles/ella-nilsen) , [Hadas Gold](https://www.cnn.com/profiles/hadas-gold) 12 min ago PUBLISHED An Amazon Web Services data center in Stone Ridge, Virginia. Northern Virginia is the largest data center market in the world, but is facing headwinds from availability of land and electric power. Nathan Howard/Getty Images [See all topics](/follow) [Email](mailto:?subject=CNN%20content%20share&body=Check%20out%20this%20article%3A%0Ahttps%3A%2F%2Fwww.cnn.com%2F2026%2F04%2F23%2Fbusiness%2Fai-compute-power-electricity-grid "Share with email") Link Copied! Tech companies charging ahead with artificial intelligence have a problem: AI’s rapid growth is colliding headlong with a finite amount of available energy and computing power. AI is evolving beyond chatbots and into autonomous AI agents that demand far more computing power and electricity, leaving companies scrambling for more energy. OpenAI recently shuttered its video-generating app Sora, in part because it was gobbling up the company’s computational supplies. The data centers that make up the backbone of AI technology need loads of energy – both to keep their servers humming and to prevent overheating. But the United States’ electrical grid [isn’t exactly up to the task](https://www.cnn.com/2024/10/18/climate/us-electric-grid-extreme-weather-climate) of powering the 21st century economy. It’s old and janky – a loosely connected collection of three grids: East, West and Texas. Experts have long warned that it is outdated and ill-equipped to respond to everything from severe weather to AI’s insatiable new power demand. Ad Feedback “Basically, we have run out of headroom, largely speaking, in the US,” said Ben Hertz-Shargel, an expert on electrification and data centers at energy research firm Wood Mackenzie. “There is a land grab happening, where companies believe that access to more capacity for compute will be necessary to win the future battle over AI services.” Companies like Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta are ramping up investments in data centers and electricity generation to power new AI models and services, betting that more energy will be needed to keep up with future AI-drive products. OpenAI last year warned the White House of an “electron gap” that puts US leadership in AI at risk, [writing that](https://openai.com/global-affairs/seizing-the-ai-opportunity/) “electrons are the new oil.” Cables sit inside the Microsoft data center campus’ Data Hall. Audrey Richardson/Reuters Elon Musk, CEO of xAI, Tesla and SpaceX, said at the World Economic Forum in January that he expects that more chips will be produced than can be turned on because of power constraints. A Google ...
